name: Retro Arcade
description: An 8-bit side-scrolling platformer presented inside a virtual arcade cabinet with full CRT screen effects, chiptune audio, high scores, and classic arcade mechanics. One demo level with a Mario-style character that runs, jumps, breaks bricks, stomps enemies, and collects power-ups.

features.title: Dev server starts and serves the app on localhost
features.description: Set up the Next.js project scaffolding so that running npm run dev starts a development server that serves a page at http://localhost:3000. The page should render without errors. Create the basic Next.js project structure with app/page.tsx as the entry point, app/layout.tsx for the root layout, and a minimal globals.css. The page.tsx should render a simple div with the text "Retro Arcade" as a placeholder. The layout.tsx should set the page title to "Retro Arcade" and use a dark background color (#000000) as the default. Install all necessary dependencies (next, react, react-dom) and configure package.json with the dev script. TypeScript should be configured via tsconfig.json.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- Running npm run dev starts the Next.js development server without errors
- Navigating to http://localhost:3000 loads the page
- The page displays the text "Retro Arcade"
- No JavaScript errors appear in the browser console
- The page background is black (#000000)
- package.json contains a "dev" script that runs Next.js
- TypeScript is configured and compiles without errors

features.title: Client-side data persists across page refresh via localStorage
features.description: Verify that the application can store and retrieve data using the browser localStorage API, and that this data survives page refreshes. Create a utility module at lib/game/storage.ts that provides helper functions for saving and loading JSON data to localStorage with a key prefix of "retro-arcade:". The module should export saveData(key: string, data: any) and loadData<T>(key: string): T | null functions. The saveData function should JSON.stringify the data before storing. The loadData function should JSON.parse the stored string and return null if the key does not exist or parsing fails. This module will be used later for high score persistence.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- lib/game/storage.ts exports saveData and loadData functions
- saveData stores JSON-stringified data to localStorage with "retro-arcade:" prefix
- loadData retrieves and parses stored data, returning the parsed object
- loadData returns null when the key does not exist
- loadData returns null when stored data is invalid JSON (does not throw)
- Data stored via saveData survives a page refresh and is retrievable via loadData
- Multiple different keys can be stored independently

features.title: No mock data patterns exist in the codebase
features.description: Ensure the codebase does not contain any hardcoded mock data, placeholder data, or dummy data patterns that would indicate fake or temporary implementations. All data displayed in the application should come from actual game logic, actual level data, or actual user interactions � not from mock objects, fixture files, or TODO placeholders. Grep the codebase for common mock patterns like "mock", "dummy", "placeholder", "lorem ipsum", "TODO", "FIXME", "HACK" and ensure none exist in production code (test files are exempt).
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- No files contain the word "mock" in variable names or comments (excluding test files)
- No "dummy" or "placeholder" data objects exist
- No "lorem ipsum" text appears anywhere
- No TODO or FIXME comments remain in production code
- All displayed content comes from real game logic or level data
- No hardcoded fake user data or scores exist

features.title: App renders without any JavaScript console errors
features.description: The application should load and render completely without producing any errors, warnings, or unhandled exceptions in the browser console. This includes no React hydration mismatches, no missing dependency warnings, no failed network requests, and no undefined reference errors. The page should reach a fully interactive state. When opening the browser developer tools console, it should be clean � no red error messages and no yellow warning messages related to the application code (browser extension warnings are acceptable).
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- Page loads at http://localhost:3000 without any console errors
- No React hydration mismatch warnings
- No unhandled promise rejections
- No undefined or null reference errors
- No failed network requests (404s, 500s)
- No TypeScript runtime errors
- Page reaches fully interactive state within 3 seconds

features.title: HTML5 Canvas element renders centered on the page
features.description:
Create a React client component at components/ArcadeCabinet.tsx that renders an HTML5 Canvas element. This component must use the "use client" directive since it accesses browser APIs. Use useRef to get a reference to the canvas DOM element.

The canvas should have a native resolution of 256x240 pixels (matching the NES resolution) but be scaled up to fill a reasonable portion of the viewport using CSS (e.g., max-height: 80vh with aspect-ratio preserved via the CSS aspect-ratio property or by calculating width from height). The canvas should be centered both horizontally and vertically on the page using flexbox on the parent container.

The background of the canvas should be filled with a dark blue color (#1a1a2e) using the 2D rendering context in a useEffect. Import and render this component from app/page.tsx. The page itself should have a black (#000000) background. The canvas element should have an id of "game-canvas" for easy reference.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- components/ArcadeCabinet.tsx exists with "use client" directive
- A canvas element with id "game-canvas" renders on the page
- Canvas has native width=256 and height=240 attributes
- Canvas is scaled up via CSS while preserving 256:240 aspect ratio
- Canvas is centered horizontally and vertically in the viewport
- Canvas background is filled with dark blue (#1a1a2e)
- Page background is black (#000000)
- useRef is used to access the canvas DOM element
- No console errors when the component mounts

features.title: Game loop runs at consistent frame rate via requestAnimationFrame
features.description:
Create the core game loop in lib/game/engine.ts. The engine should export a GameEngine class that takes a canvas element reference in its constructor and stores the 2D rendering context.

Implement a game loop using requestAnimationFrame that runs update(deltaTime) and render(ctx) methods each frame. Calculate deltaTime as the time elapsed since the last frame in seconds. Cap deltaTime at 1/30 (about 0.033s) to prevent physics explosion when the tab loses focus and regains it after a long pause.

Provide start() and stop() methods. start() begins the requestAnimationFrame loop and records the initial timestamp. stop() calls cancelAnimationFrame to halt the loop. Store the animation frame ID so it can be cancelled.

The ArcadeCabinet component should instantiate the GameEngine in a useEffect hook, call start(), and return a cleanup function that calls stop(). For now, the render method should clear the canvas with clearRect and fill it with the dark blue background color (#1a1a2e). The update method can be empty.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- lib/game/engine.ts exports a GameEngine class
- Constructor accepts a canvas element and stores the 2D context
- Game loop uses requestAnimationFrame, not setInterval
- deltaTime is calculated in seconds between frames
- deltaTime is capped at 1/30 second maximum
- start() begins the loop, stop() cancels it via cancelAnimationFrame
- Canvas is cleared and filled with background color each frame
- ArcadeCabinet.tsx creates GameEngine in useEffect with cleanup on unmount
- No memory leaks when component unmounts and remounts

features.title: Game state machine manages attract, playing, paused, game-over, and high-score states
features.description:
Add a state machine to the GameEngine in lib/game/engine.ts (or create a separate lib/game/state.ts and import it). Define a TypeScript enum or string union type GameState with values: ATTRACT, PLAYING, PAUSED, GAME_OVER, HIGH_SCORE_ENTRY.

The GameEngine should have a currentState property initialized to ATTRACT. Add a setState(newState: GameState) method that updates currentState. The update() and render() methods should use a switch statement on currentState to dispatch to state-specific logic.

For now, each state should render a visually distinct screen so you can verify transitions work:
- ATTRACT: dark blue background with white text "ATTRACT MODE" centered
- PLAYING: dark green background with white text "PLAYING"
- PAUSED: gray background with white text "PAUSED"
- GAME_OVER: dark red background with white text "GAME OVER"
- HIGH_SCORE_ENTRY: dark purple background with white text "HIGH SCORES"

Use ctx.fillText to render the state labels. Set font to a monospace font at 16px. This state machine is the backbone that all future gameplay features plug into.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- GameState type exists with ATTRACT, PLAYING, PAUSED, GAME_OVER, HIGH_SCORE_ENTRY values
- GameEngine has a currentState property defaulting to ATTRACT
- setState method transitions between states
- update() dispatches to state-specific update logic via switch
- render() dispatches to state-specific render logic via switch
- Each state renders a different background color and label text
- Text is centered on the canvas using measureText for horizontal centering
- State transitions do not cause errors or memory leaks
- Game always starts in ATTRACT state on page load

features.title: Keyboard input handler captures arrow keys, WASD, space, and escape
features.description:
Create an input handling module at lib/game/input.ts. Export an InputManager class that tracks keyboard state.

In the constructor (or a setup() method), add keydown and keyup event listeners on the document. Maintain two data structures: a Set or Map called keysDown that tracks which keys are currently held, and a Set called keysJustPressed that tracks which keys transitioned from up to down this frame (rising edge detection).

Track these keys: ArrowLeft, ArrowRight, ArrowUp, ArrowDown, a, w, s, d, ' ' (space), Escape, Enter, p. On keydown, add the key to keysDown and keysJustPressed. On keyup, remove from keysDown.

Provide these methods:
- isKeyDown(key: string): boolean — returns true while a key is held
- wasKeyPressed(key: string): boolean — returns true only on the first frame a key goes down
- resetFrameState(): void — clears keysJustPressed; call this at the end of each game loop frame
- setup(): void — adds event listeners
- teardown(): void — removes event listeners

Call event.preventDefault() in the keydown handler for game keys to prevent the browser from scrolling when pressing arrow keys or space. The GameEngine should create an InputManager instance, call setup() on start, teardown() on stop, and call resetFrameState() at the end of each frame.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- lib/game/input.ts exports an InputManager class
- Tracks keydown/keyup state for ArrowLeft, ArrowRight, ArrowUp, ArrowDown, a, w, s, d, space, Escape, Enter, p
- isKeyDown(key) returns true while a key is held down
- isKeyDown(key) returns false after the key is released
- wasKeyPressed(key) returns true only on the first frame a key transitions to down
- wasKeyPressed(key) returns false on subsequent frames even if key is still held
- resetFrameState() clears the just-pressed set
- event.preventDefault() is called for game keys to prevent page scrolling
- setup() adds event listeners on document
- teardown() removes event listeners cleanly
- GameEngine integrates InputManager into the game loop

features.title: Player 8-bit sprite renders on canvas using pixel color array
features.description:
Create a sprite system at lib/game/sprites.ts that defines 8-bit pixel art sprites as 2D arrays of color values. Each sprite is a number[][] where each number maps to a color in a palette array, and 0 means transparent.

Define a color palette as an array of hex color strings, e.g.: ['transparent', '#e8210a' (red), '#f0b088' (skin), '#0048e8' (blue), '#6b3e08' (brown), '#f8f8f8' (white)]. Define the player sprite as a 16x16 grid of palette indices forming a recognizable humanoid character: a red hat/cap on top, a skin-colored face with simple eyes, a blue shirt/overalls body, and brown shoes at the bottom. Keep the design simple but recognizable.

Create a renderSprite(ctx: CanvasRenderingContext2D, sprite: number[][], palette: string[], x: number, y: number, scale: number) function that iterates over the 2D array and draws a filled rectangle of size (scale x scale) for each non-zero cell, using the palette color.

Create a Player class in lib/game/player.ts with x, y, width, height properties. Width and height should be 16 (the sprite size). In the PLAYING state render logic of the GameEngine, create a Player instance at position (32, 200) and call renderSprite to draw the player sprite at (player.x, player.y) with scale=1.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- lib/game/sprites.ts exports sprite data and a renderSprite function
- A color palette array maps indices to hex color strings
- Player sprite is defined as a 16x16 grid of palette indices
- The sprite depicts a humanoid character with hat, face, body, and shoes
- The sprite uses at least 4 distinct colors
- renderSprite draws each non-zero pixel as a filled rectangle
- renderSprite respects the scale parameter for pixel size
- Zero/transparent pixels are not drawn
- lib/game/player.ts exports a Player class with x, y, width, height
- Player sprite renders visibly on the canvas in PLAYING state
- The character is recognizable as a small humanoid figure

features.title: Player moves left and right with arrow keys and WASD
features.description:
Add horizontal movement to the Player class in lib/game/player.ts. Add a vx (velocity x) property initialized to 0, and a constant MOVE_SPEED (define in lib/game/constants.ts, value: 80 pixels per second).

In the Player's update(dt, input) method, check the InputManager: if ArrowLeft or 'a' is down, set vx = -MOVE_SPEED. If ArrowRight or 'd' is down, set vx = MOVE_SPEED. If neither is down, set vx = 0. Then update x: this.x += this.vx * dt.

Add a facing direction property ('left' or 'right') that updates based on movement direction. When rendering, if facing left, flip the sprite horizontally by iterating the sprite rows in reverse column order (or by using ctx.save/scale(-1,1)/translate and ctx.restore).

Create lib/game/constants.ts to hold all game constants. Start with PLAYER_MOVE_SPEED = 80. The GameEngine should call player.update(dt, inputManager) in the PLAYING state update, and render the player sprite in the PLAYING state render.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- Player has a vx property and uses PLAYER_MOVE_SPEED from constants.ts
- Pressing ArrowLeft or 'a' moves the player left
- Pressing ArrowRight or 'd' moves the player right
- Releasing movement keys stops horizontal movement
- Player position updates smoothly based on deltaTime
- Player has a facing direction that changes with movement
- Sprite flips horizontally when facing left
- lib/game/constants.ts exists with PLAYER_MOVE_SPEED = 80
- Movement feels responsive with no input lag

features.title: Gravity pulls player down each frame
features.description:
Add vertical physics to the Player class. Add a vy (velocity y) property initialized to 0 and an onGround boolean initialized to false. Define GRAVITY = 600 and MAX_FALL_SPEED = 300 in constants.ts (pixels per second and pixels per second respectively).

In player.update(dt, input), apply gravity: this.vy += GRAVITY * dt. Cap vy at MAX_FALL_SPEED so the player does not accelerate infinitely. Then update y: this.y += this.vy * dt.

For now, add a temporary ground check: if player.y + player.height > 224 (near the bottom of the 240-pixel canvas), set player.y = 224 - player.height, player.vy = 0, and player.onGround = true. Otherwise set onGround = false. This temporary floor will be replaced by proper tile collision in a later feature.

The player should visibly fall when spawned above the ground level and stop when hitting the temporary floor.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- Player has vy property starting at 0
- GRAVITY (600) and MAX_FALL_SPEED (300) are defined in constants.ts
- Gravity increases vy each frame: vy += GRAVITY * dt
- vy is capped at MAX_FALL_SPEED
- Player y position updates: y += vy * dt
- Temporary ground collision stops the player at y = 224 - height
- Player onGround is true when resting on the temporary floor
- Player onGround is false when in the air
- Player visibly falls and lands when the game starts

features.title: Player stands on ground and platform tiles via collision detection
features.description:
Create a physics/collision module at lib/game/physics.ts that implements AABB (axis-aligned bounding box) collision detection between the player and the tile map.

Create a function resolveCollisions(player, tileMap, tileSize) that checks if the player's bounding box overlaps any solid tiles. The approach:
1. Calculate which tiles the player overlaps using integer division of player bounds by tileSize
2. For each overlapping solid tile, calculate the overlap on each axis
3. Resolve by pushing the player out on the axis of minimum penetration
4. For downward collisions (player landing on top of a tile), set player.vy = 0 and player.onGround = true
5. For upward collisions (player hitting head on tile above), set player.vy = 0
6. For horizontal collisions, set player.vx = 0 and stop horizontal movement

Remove the temporary ground check from the player. Instead, call resolveCollisions after updating the player position each frame. The player should now stand on any solid tile in the level, not just the hardcoded y=224 floor. This requires the tile-based level (feature 14) to exist, so mark it as a dependency.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- lib/game/physics.ts exports a resolveCollisions function
- AABB collision detection checks player against solid tiles in the tile map
- Player stops when landing on top of a solid tile (vy = 0, onGround = true)
- Player stops when hitting head on a tile above (vy = 0)
- Player stops when walking into a solid tile horizontally
- The temporary hardcoded ground check is removed
- Player correctly stands on platforms at various heights
- Player does not fall through single-tile-wide platforms
- Collision resolution handles corners without getting stuck

features.title: Player jumps with spacebar when grounded with no double-jump
features.description:
Add jumping to the Player class. Define JUMP_VELOCITY = 250 in constants.ts (pixels per second, upward).

In player.update(), check the InputManager: if space (or 'w' or ArrowUp) wasKeyPressed AND player.onGround is true, set player.vy = -JUMP_VELOCITY (negative because y-axis points down in canvas). Also set player.onGround = false immediately to prevent multi-frame jumps.

The jump should feel responsive: the player launches upward immediately on key press, arcs through the air due to gravity, and lands back on the ground. Double-jumping must NOT be possible — pressing space while airborne does nothing.

Optionally add variable jump height: if the player releases the jump key while still moving upward (vy < 0), cut vy in half. This gives a short hop on a tap and a full jump on a hold, which feels much better for platforming.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- JUMP_VELOCITY = 250 is defined in constants.ts
- Pressing space/w/ArrowUp while onGround makes the player jump
- Jump sets vy = -JUMP_VELOCITY and onGround = false
- Player cannot jump while already in the air (no double jump)
- Player follows a natural arc: rises, slows, falls back down
- Player lands on the ground and can jump again
- Optional: releasing jump key early reduces vy for variable jump height
- Jump feels responsive with no delay between keypress and movement

features.title: Tile-based level renders from 2D number array with ground and platforms
features.description:
Create a level system at lib/game/level.ts and lib/game/data/level1.ts. Define the level as a 2D number array (15 rows by 64 columns for a horizontally scrolling level). Each number represents a tile type.

Define tile types in constants.ts: TILE_EMPTY = 0, TILE_GROUND = 1, TILE_BRICK = 2, TILE_QUESTION = 3, TILE_PIPE_TOP_LEFT = 4, TILE_PIPE_TOP_RIGHT = 5, TILE_PIPE_BODY_LEFT = 6, TILE_PIPE_BODY_RIGHT = 7, TILE_PLATFORM = 8.

Define TILE_SIZE = 16 in constants.ts. Create a tile palette mapping tile types to colors for rendering: ground = brown (#8B4513), brick = dark red (#A0522D), question = gold (#FFD700), pipe = green (#228B22), platform = gray (#808080). Empty tiles are not drawn.

Create a renderLevel(ctx, tileMap, tileSize, cameraX) function that iterates the 2D array and draws each non-empty tile as a filled rectangle at the correct position, offset by cameraX for scrolling. Only render tiles that are within the visible canvas area (256 pixels wide) for performance.

Design level1.ts as a fun Mario-style level: flat ground along the bottom 2 rows, some gaps to jump over, floating platforms at various heights, some brick and question blocks, and a pipe or two. The level should be 64 tiles wide (1024 pixels).
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- lib/game/level.ts exports renderLevel and tile type constants
- lib/game/data/level1.ts exports a 2D number array (15 rows x 64 columns)
- TILE_SIZE = 16 is defined in constants.ts
- Each tile type renders as a distinct colored rectangle
- Empty tiles (0) are not drawn
- renderLevel only draws tiles visible within the canvas viewport
- cameraX parameter offsets tile rendering for horizontal scrolling
- Level 1 has ground, gaps, floating platforms, brick blocks, question blocks, and pipes
- The level is visually recognizable as a side-scrolling platformer layout

features.title: Camera follows player horizontally and level scrolls
features.description:
Create a camera module at lib/game/camera.ts. Export a Camera class with an x property (the left edge of the viewport in world coordinates) and a width property (256, the canvas width).

Add an update(playerX, playerWidth, levelWidthPixels) method. The camera should follow the player such that the player stays roughly centered horizontally. Specifically: set camera.x = playerX + playerWidth/2 - camera.width/2. Clamp camera.x so it never goes below 0 or beyond levelWidthPixels - camera.width. This prevents showing empty space beyond the level boundaries.

In the GameEngine, create a Camera instance. Call camera.update() in the PLAYING state update. Pass camera.x to renderLevel() as the cameraX offset. When rendering the player, subtract camera.x from the player's world x position to convert to screen coordinates. All entities (enemies, items, blocks) will also need this camera offset when rendering, but those are handled in their own features.

The result: as the player moves right, the level scrolls left. The player cannot scroll past the level start or end.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- lib/game/camera.ts exports a Camera class with x and width properties
- Camera centers on the player horizontally
- Camera x is clamped to prevent showing area beyond level boundaries
- Camera x never goes below 0
- Camera x never goes beyond levelWidth - cameraWidth
- Level tiles scroll as the player moves right
- Player renders at correct screen position (world x minus camera x)
- Scrolling is smooth with no jitter or snapping

features.title: Background layer with parallax scrolling effect
features.description:
Add a background rendering layer that scrolls at a slower rate than the foreground level, creating a parallax depth effect. Create a renderBackground(ctx, cameraX) function in lib/game/level.ts or a new lib/game/background.ts.

The background should consist of:
1. A gradient sky from dark blue (#0a0a2e) at the top to lighter blue (#1a1a4e) at the bottom
2. Simple pixel-art mountains or hills as silhouettes at the bottom third of the screen, rendered as filled polygons in a medium blue (#2a2a5e)
3. Small white dots scattered in the upper portion as stars

The mountains/hills should scroll at 0.3x the camera speed (cameraX * 0.3), and the stars should scroll at 0.1x the camera speed, creating a layered depth effect.

Call renderBackground() before renderLevel() in the PLAYING state render, so the background appears behind the tiles. The parallax effect should be subtle but noticeable as the player moves through the level.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- Background renders behind the level tiles
- Sky gradient goes from dark blue at top to lighter blue at bottom
- Mountain/hill silhouettes appear in the lower portion
- Small star dots appear in the upper portion
- Mountains scroll at 0.3x the camera speed (parallax)
- Stars scroll at 0.1x the camera speed (slower parallax)
- Parallax creates a visible depth effect when moving
- Background does not obscure foreground tiles
- No visual artifacts or gaps in the background

features.title: Breakable bricks shatter when hit from below with particle effect
features.description:
Implement breakable brick logic. When the player jumps and hits a brick tile (TILE_BRICK) from below (player's head collides with the bottom of a brick tile), the brick should be destroyed.

In physics.ts or a new lib/game/blocks.ts, detect when the player collides with a brick tile from below (player is moving upward, collision is on the top edge of the player). When this happens:
1. Remove the brick from the tile map by setting that cell to TILE_EMPTY
2. Create a particle effect: spawn 4 small square particles at the brick's position, each moving outward in a different diagonal direction with some random velocity. The particles should be brown/brick-colored, fall due to gravity, and disappear after 0.5 seconds.

Create a simple particle system in lib/game/particles.ts. Export a ParticleManager class that can spawn particles and update/render them each frame. Each particle has x, y, vx, vy, color, and lifetime properties. The update method applies gravity to vy and decreases lifetime. The render method draws each particle as a small (3x3 pixel) filled rectangle. Remove particles when their lifetime reaches 0.

The GameEngine should create a ParticleManager and call its update/render in the PLAYING state.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- Hitting a TILE_BRICK from below destroys it (sets tile to TILE_EMPTY)
- Brick only breaks when player hits it from below (head collision while jumping)
- Walking into a brick from the side does NOT break it
- 4 particle fragments spawn at the brick's position on break
- Particles move outward in diagonal directions
- Particles fall due to gravity
- Particles disappear after approximately 0.5 seconds
- lib/game/particles.ts exports a ParticleManager class
- ParticleManager updates and renders all active particles
- Breaking a brick feels satisfying with visible debris

features.title: Question blocks release item when hit and change to used appearance
features.description:
Implement question block behavior. Question blocks (TILE_QUESTION) behave like bricks in that the player activates them by hitting from below, but instead of breaking, they release an item and change to a 'used' state.

Add a new tile type TILE_QUESTION_USED = 9 in constants.ts. This tile renders as a darker, flat brown (#8B7355) instead of the gold color, indicating it has been used.

When the player hits a TILE_QUESTION from below:
1. Change the tile to TILE_QUESTION_USED in the tile map
2. Spawn an item above the block. For now, spawn a coin that pops up with a small animation (rises 8 pixels, then disappears) and adds to the score. The coin should be rendered as a small yellow (#FFD700) circle or square.
3. Add a small bounce animation to the block: shift it up by 4 pixels briefly, then back to its original position over about 0.1 seconds.

The question block should have a simple visual indicator that it is active: render it as a gold square with a white question mark (?) drawn on it using ctx.fillText, or just a gold square with a darker gold border.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- TILE_QUESTION_USED tile type exists and renders as darker brown
- Hitting a question block from below changes it to TILE_QUESTION_USED
- A coin pops up from the block when hit
- The coin rises briefly then disappears
- Score increases when the coin from a question block is collected
- Question block bounces slightly when hit (visual feedback)
- Used question blocks cannot be hit again (no item, no bounce)
- Question blocks render with a visually distinct gold appearance
- Side collisions with question blocks do not activate them

features.title: Collectible coins placed in level disappear when touched by player
features.description:
Add collectible coins throughout the level. Create a coin entity system in lib/game/items.ts. Coins are placed at specific positions defined in the level data. Add a coins array to the level data structure, where each coin has an x, y position in tile coordinates.

In level1.ts, add 15-20 coins placed at interesting locations: floating in the air requiring jumps to reach, in lines along platforms, and above gaps as guides for the player.

Each coin renders as a small (8x8 pixel) yellow (#FFD700) square with a slightly darker border, or a simple circle. Coins should have a simple animation: oscillate vertically by 2 pixels in a sine wave pattern to make them look alive.

When the player's bounding box overlaps a coin's bounding box, the coin disappears (remove from the active coins array). This collision check runs each frame in the update loop. The score increment and sound effect for coin collection will be handled in separate features.

Render coins with camera offset so they scroll with the level.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- lib/game/items.ts exports coin-related classes and functions
- Level 1 data includes 15-20 coin positions
- Coins render as small yellow squares or circles (8x8 pixels)
- Coins have a subtle bobbing animation (vertical sine wave)
- Player touching a coin makes it disappear
- Coin collision uses AABB overlap detection
- Coins scroll correctly with the camera
- Coins are placed at interesting platforming locations in the level
- Collecting all coins is possible by playing through the level

features.title: Level has start position and end goal with flagpole or door
features.description:
Add a level start position and end goal to the level system. In level1.ts, define a startPosition object with x and y coordinates (in pixels) where the player spawns. The player should spawn near the left side of the level, on top of the ground.

At the right end of the level (around tile column 60), place a goal object. This can be a flagpole or a door. For a flagpole: render a vertical gray (#C0C0C0) pole (2 pixels wide, 48 pixels tall) with a small green (#00FF00) triangular flag at the top. The flag should wave slightly (shift 1-2 pixels horizontally in a sine pattern).

When the player reaches the goal (player x + width >= goal x), trigger level completion. For now, transition to the GAME_OVER state (a proper completion screen will be added later). The player should stop receiving input during the completion transition.

Define the goal position in the level data alongside the tile map and coin positions. The GameEngine reads startPosition to place the player at level start.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- Level data includes a startPosition with x, y coordinates
- Player spawns at the startPosition when the game starts
- A flagpole or door renders near the right end of the level
- The flagpole is visually distinct (gray pole, green flag)
- The flag has a subtle waving animation
- Player reaching the goal triggers level completion
- On completion, game transitions away from PLAYING state
- Player input is disabled during the completion moment
- Goal is rendered with camera offset (scrolls with level)

features.title: Enemy sprite renders and patrols back and forth reversing at edges
features.description:
Create an enemy system at lib/game/enemies.ts. Define an Enemy class with x, y, vx, width (16), height (16), and active (boolean) properties. Define ENEMY_SPEED = 30 in constants.ts.

Define an enemy sprite in sprites.ts as a 16x16 pixel art creature. Keep it simple: a round or mushroom-shaped body in a distinct color (e.g., brown #8B4513 body with tan #DEB887 feet and angry white eyes with black pupils). The enemy should look distinct from the player.

In the level data, add an enemies array with spawn positions for 5-8 enemies placed throughout the level on ground or platforms. Each enemy initializes with vx = ENEMY_SPEED.

Enemy update logic:
1. Move horizontally: x += vx * dt
2. Check the tile below and ahead of the enemy. If the next tile ahead is empty (a cliff edge) or if the enemy hits a solid wall tile, reverse vx (vx = -vx). This makes enemies patrol back and forth on their platform.

Render enemies with camera offset. Flip the sprite horizontally based on movement direction, same as the player. Inactive enemies (active=false) are not updated or rendered.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- lib/game/enemies.ts exports an Enemy class
- Enemy sprite is defined in sprites.ts as a 16x16 pixel art creature
- Enemy sprite is visually distinct from the player sprite
- ENEMY_SPEED = 30 is defined in constants.ts
- Level data includes 5-8 enemy spawn positions
- Enemies patrol horizontally at ENEMY_SPEED
- Enemies reverse direction when reaching a platform edge
- Enemies reverse direction when hitting a wall tile
- Enemies render with correct camera offset
- Enemy sprite flips based on movement direction
- Inactive enemies are not updated or rendered

features.title: Player takes damage when touching enemy from the side
features.description:
Implement player-enemy side collision. Each frame during the PLAYING state, check if the player's bounding box overlaps any active enemy's bounding box. If an overlap is detected and the player is NOT landing on top of the enemy (handled in the stomp feature), the player takes damage.

When the player takes damage:
1. Decrement the player's lives count (lives is tracked on the player or in a game state object)
2. Make the player briefly invincible for 2 seconds (set an invincibleTimer). During invincibility, the player sprite flashes (alternate between visible and hidden every 0.1 seconds). Enemy collisions are ignored during invincibility.
3. Knock the player back slightly: set vx to the opposite direction of the enemy (push away) at 100 pixels/sec, and set vy to -150 (a small upward bounce).

If the player has 0 lives remaining after taking damage, trigger GAME_OVER state. The lives system starts the player with 3 lives (define STARTING_LIVES = 3 in constants.ts).

Do NOT handle stomp/top collision here — that is a separate feature. Only trigger damage when the horizontal overlap is significant and the player is not clearly above the enemy.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- Player-enemy collision is detected each frame using AABB overlap
- Side/horizontal contact with an enemy damages the player
- Player loses one life on damage
- Player becomes invincible for 2 seconds after taking damage
- During invincibility, player sprite flashes visibly
- Enemy collisions are ignored during invincibility period
- Player is knocked back away from the enemy on damage
- Player gets a small upward bounce on damage
- STARTING_LIVES = 3 is defined in constants.ts
- If lives reach 0, game transitions to GAME_OVER state

features.title: Player stomps enemy when landing on top with squish animation
features.description:
Implement enemy stomping. When the player lands on top of an enemy (player is falling downward, player's bottom edge overlaps enemy's top edge, and the horizontal overlap is significant), the enemy is defeated instead of damaging the player.

Detection logic: during player-enemy collision, if the player's vy > 0 (falling) AND the player's previous y + height was above the enemy's y (the player was above the enemy last frame), treat it as a stomp rather than a side collision.

When an enemy is stomped:
1. Set enemy.active = false so it stops updating and rendering
2. Give the player a small upward bounce: set player.vy = -STOMP_BOUNCE (define STOMP_BOUNCE = 150 in constants.ts)
3. Play a brief squish animation: before deactivating, briefly render the enemy sprite compressed to half height for 0.1 seconds, then remove it. Alternatively, spawn a small particle burst.
4. Add points to the score (100 points per stomp, tracked in a score variable).

The stomp check must run BEFORE the side-damage check so that landing on an enemy does not damage the player.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- Landing on top of an enemy while falling defeats the enemy
- Stomp detection checks that player is falling (vy > 0) and above the enemy
- Stomped enemy is deactivated (stops moving and rendering)
- Player bounces upward after a stomp (vy = -STOMP_BOUNCE)
- STOMP_BOUNCE = 150 is defined in constants.ts
- A brief squish visual plays when enemy is stomped
- Score increases by 100 on stomp
- Stomp check runs before side-damage check
- Player is never damaged when clearly landing on top of enemy
- Multiple enemies can be stomped in sequence (chain stomping)

features.title: HUD overlay shows score, lives count, and coin count
features.description:
Create a HUD (heads-up display) module at lib/game/hud.ts that renders game information on top of the gameplay. The HUD should be drawn AFTER all game entities so it appears on top.

Render the following information using ctx.fillText with a monospace pixel-style font:
- Top-left corner: "SCORE" label on line 1, actual score value below it (e.g., "000350")
- Top-center: "COINS" label with a small yellow square icon, coin count (e.g., "x07")
- Top-right corner: "LIVES" label with a small red heart icon, lives count (e.g., "x3")

Use white (#FFFFFF) text on a semi-transparent black background strip across the top 20 pixels of the canvas. The font should be small (8px or 10px) to fit the 256-pixel-wide canvas. Score should be zero-padded to 6 digits.

The HUD reads from a game state object that tracks score, coins, and lives. Create this state object (or add properties to GameEngine): score (number, starts at 0), coins (number, starts at 0), lives (number, starts at 3).

The HUD renders in the PLAYING state only and is NOT affected by camera scrolling (it stays fixed on screen).
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- lib/game/hud.ts exports a renderHUD function
- Score displays in top-left with zero-padded 6 digits
- Coin count displays in top-center with a yellow icon
- Lives count displays in top-right with a heart icon
- HUD text uses a monospace font and is readable at canvas resolution
- Semi-transparent dark background strip behind HUD text
- HUD is drawn on top of all game entities
- HUD does not scroll with the camera (fixed position)
- Score, coins, and lives are tracked in a game state object
- HUD only renders during PLAYING state

features.title: Score increases from coins, enemy stomps, and brick breaks
features.description:
Wire up the scoring system so that score increases from gameplay actions. The score is tracked as a number on the game state object (initialized to 0 at game start).

Points values (define in constants.ts):
- COIN_POINTS = 50: awarded when the player collects a coin (overlaps a coin entity)
- STOMP_POINTS = 100: awarded when the player stomps an enemy
- BRICK_POINTS = 10: awarded when the player breaks a brick block
- QUESTION_COIN_POINTS = 100: awarded when hitting a question block that releases a coin

In each relevant system (coin collection in items.ts, enemy stomp in enemies.ts, brick break in blocks.ts/physics.ts), add the appropriate points to the game state score. The HUD already displays the score, so after this feature the displayed score should update in real-time as the player collects coins, stomps enemies, and breaks bricks.

Also increment the coins counter in game state when collecting a coin (both level coins and question block coins).
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- COIN_POINTS, STOMP_POINTS, BRICK_POINTS, QUESTION_COIN_POINTS defined in constants.ts
- Collecting a coin adds 50 to score and increments coin counter
- Stomping an enemy adds 100 to score
- Breaking a brick adds 10 to score
- Hitting a question block adds 100 to score and increments coin counter
- Score updates are visible on the HUD in real-time
- Coin counter on the HUD updates when coins are collected
- Score starts at 0 at the beginning of each game
- Score accumulates correctly across multiple actions

features.title: Lives system with 3 lives, life loss on damage, and respawn at level start
features.description:
Formalize the lives system. The player starts each game with 3 lives (STARTING_LIVES in constants.ts). Lives are displayed on the HUD.

When the player takes damage from an enemy (side collision) and is not invincible:
1. Decrement lives by 1
2. If lives > 0: make player invincible for 2 seconds, knock them back, and continue playing. Reset the player position to the level start position (respawn).
3. If lives <= 0: transition to GAME_OVER state

Also handle falling into pits: if the player's y position goes below the canvas height (y > 240), treat it as a death. Decrement lives, and either respawn at start (if lives > 0) or game over (if lives = 0). Pit deaths should be instant (no invincibility needed since the player respawns).

When respawning, reset the player's position to the level startPosition, reset velocity to 0, and briefly make the player invincible. Keep the score and coin count — they are not reset on death.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- Player starts with 3 lives at the beginning of each game
- Taking enemy damage reduces lives by 1
- Falling below the screen (into a pit) reduces lives by 1
- With lives > 0 after damage, player respawns at level start position
- Player velocity is reset to 0 on respawn
- Score and coin count are preserved across deaths
- Player is briefly invincible after respawning
- With lives = 0, game transitions to GAME_OVER state
- Lives counter on HUD updates correctly after each death

features.title: Game over screen displays when lives reach zero
features.description:
Implement the GAME_OVER state rendering and behavior. When the game transitions to GAME_OVER state (triggered by the lives system when lives reach 0), display a game over screen.

The game over screen should render on the canvas:
1. A dark semi-transparent overlay on top of the last game frame (or just a solid dark red/black background)
2. Large text "GAME OVER" centered on screen in white, using a bigger font size (e.g., 16px or rendered as large pixel art letters)
3. Below that, show the final score: "SCORE: XXXXXX"
4. Below that, show "PRESS ENTER TO CONTINUE" in smaller text, flashing on and off every 0.5 seconds

When the player presses Enter in the GAME_OVER state, transition to HIGH_SCORE_ENTRY state (where they can enter their initials). If they press Escape, transition back to ATTRACT state (title screen) instead.

The game over screen should feel dramatic — consider a brief pause (0.5 second delay) before showing the text, as if the game is processing the defeat.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- GAME_OVER state renders a distinct game over screen
- "GAME OVER" text is prominently displayed and centered
- Final score is shown below the game over text
- "PRESS ENTER TO CONTINUE" text flashes on and off
- Pressing Enter transitions to HIGH_SCORE_ENTRY state
- Pressing Escape transitions to ATTRACT state
- There is a brief dramatic pause before text appears
- The screen is visually distinct from other game states
- No gameplay updates occur during GAME_OVER state

features.title: High score entry screen with 3-letter initials input
features.description:
Implement the HIGH_SCORE_ENTRY state. After game over, the player enters their initials (3 letters) to record their score on the high score table.

The screen should display:
1. "NEW HIGH SCORE!" or "ENTER YOUR NAME" at the top
2. The player's final score
3. Three letter slots displayed as "A _ _" initially. The current slot is highlighted (shown in a different color or with an underline/cursor).

Controls:
- ArrowUp or 'w': cycle the current letter forward (A -> B -> C ... -> Z -> A)
- ArrowDown or 's': cycle the current letter backward (Z -> Y -> X ... -> A -> Z)
- ArrowRight or 'd' or Enter: confirm current letter and move to the next slot
- ArrowLeft or 'a': go back to the previous slot (if not on the first)
- When all 3 letters are confirmed (Enter on the third slot), save the high score and transition to ATTRACT state

The letters should be displayed large (rendered as pixel art text or large fillText). Each letter should visually update as the player cycles through the alphabet. The available characters are A-Z only (26 letters).
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- HIGH_SCORE_ENTRY state renders a distinct initials entry screen
- Three letter slots are displayed with the current slot highlighted
- ArrowUp/w cycles the current letter forward through the alphabet
- ArrowDown/s cycles the current letter backward through the alphabet
- ArrowRight/d/Enter confirms current letter and moves to next slot
- ArrowLeft/a goes back to the previous slot
- Letters wrap around (Z -> A going forward, A -> Z going backward)
- All 3 letters confirmed triggers save and transition to ATTRACT
- Player's final score is displayed on the entry screen
- Only uppercase A-Z letters are available

features.title: High score table persists to localStorage and displays top 10
features.description:
Create a high score persistence system at lib/game/highscores.ts using the storage utility from lib/game/storage.ts.

The high score table stores up to 10 entries, each with: initials (3-letter string), score (number), and date (ISO string). The table is sorted by score descending (highest first).

Export these functions:
- getHighScores(): returns the array of high score entries from localStorage (or an empty array if none exist)
- addHighScore(initials: string, score: number): adds a new entry, sorts the table, trims to top 10, and saves to localStorage
- isHighScore(score: number): returns true if the score qualifies for the top 10 (score > the 10th entry's score, or fewer than 10 entries exist)

When the player finishes entering initials in HIGH_SCORE_ENTRY, call addHighScore with their initials and final score. Before transitioning to HIGH_SCORE_ENTRY, check isHighScore — if the score does not qualify, skip the initials entry and go directly to ATTRACT.

The high score table should render as part of the ATTRACT mode cycle (handled in a later feature) and briefly on transition from HIGH_SCORE_ENTRY to ATTRACT. Show the table with rank (1-10), initials, and zero-padded score, rendered in a retro monospace style.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- lib/game/highscores.ts exports getHighScores, addHighScore, isHighScore
- High scores are stored in localStorage via the storage utility
- Table stores up to 10 entries sorted by score descending
- addHighScore adds entry, sorts, trims to 10, and persists
- isHighScore correctly identifies qualifying scores
- Non-qualifying scores skip the initials entry screen
- High scores survive page refresh (localStorage persistence)
- High score table renders with rank, initials, and formatted score
- Table uses a retro monospace visual style
- Empty table handles gracefully (no errors, shows empty state)

features.title: Power-up items emerge from question blocks and move across ground
features.description:
Extend the question block system so some question blocks release power-up items instead of coins. In the level data, specify which question blocks contain power-ups and which type. Add a questionBlockContents mapping in level1.ts that maps tile positions to content types: 'coin' (default), 'speed', 'star', 'life', 'sizeup'.

When a question block is hit from below, check its contents. If it contains a power-up:
1. Spawn a power-up entity above the block. The entity rises out of the block over 0.3 seconds (emerges smoothly, not instantly).
2. Once fully emerged, the power-up moves horizontally at POWERUP_SPEED = 40 pixels/sec in the direction the player is NOT facing (so it moves away from the player, giving them a chance to chase it).
3. The power-up falls due to gravity and slides along the ground. It reverses direction when hitting a wall (similar to enemy patrol).
4. The power-up has a bounding box matching its sprite size (16x16).

Define power-up sprites in sprites.ts: each should be a 16x16 pixel art icon. Speed = blue circle with arrow, Star = yellow star shape, Life = green mushroom, SizeUp = red mushroom. Keep designs simple but distinct.

When the player overlaps a power-up, the power-up is collected (removed from the game). The actual effects are handled in separate features.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- Question blocks can contain different power-up types based on level data
- Power-ups emerge from the block with a rising animation over 0.3 seconds
- Emerged power-ups move horizontally at POWERUP_SPEED
- Power-ups fall due to gravity and rest on ground/platforms
- Power-ups reverse direction when hitting walls
- Each power-up type has a distinct 16x16 pixel art sprite
- Player collecting a power-up (AABB overlap) removes it from the game
- POWERUP_SPEED = 40 is defined in constants.ts
- Power-ups scroll correctly with the camera
- Uncollected power-ups continue moving indefinitely

features.title: Speed boost power-up grants temporary faster movement with visual indicator
features.description:
Implement the speed boost power-up effect. When the player collects a speed boost power-up (blue circle with arrow icon):

1. Multiply the player's PLAYER_MOVE_SPEED by 1.8 for SPEED_BOOST_DURATION = 5 seconds (define in constants.ts)
2. Display a visual indicator: either the player sprite tints slightly blue, or small speed line particles trail behind the player as they move
3. Show a brief pickup text: "+SPEED" that floats upward and fades out over 0.5 seconds at the pickup location
4. After the duration expires, return to normal speed

Track the active power-up timer on the player object. Add a speedBoostTimer property that counts down each frame. While speedBoostTimer > 0, apply the speed multiplier. When it reaches 0, restore normal speed.

The HUD should show the remaining power-up duration as a small bar or icon in the top area when a power-up is active. Add 200 points to the score on pickup.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- Collecting a speed boost power-up increases movement speed by 1.8x
- SPEED_BOOST_DURATION = 5 seconds is defined in constants.ts
- Speed boost lasts exactly the defined duration then wears off
- A visual indicator shows the player has a speed boost active
- "+SPEED" floating text appears at pickup location
- Speed returns to normal after the timer expires
- Player object tracks speedBoostTimer countdown
- HUD shows an active power-up indicator during the boost
- Score increases by 200 on pickup
- Collecting another speed boost resets the timer

features.title: Invincibility star makes player flash and defeat enemies on contact
features.description:
Implement the invincibility star power-up effect. When the player collects a star power-up (yellow star icon):

1. Make the player invincible for STAR_DURATION = 8 seconds (define in constants.ts)
2. During invincibility, the player sprite cycles through rainbow colors rapidly (change the sprite palette every 0.05 seconds, cycling through red, orange, yellow, green, blue, purple). This creates the classic flashing star effect.
3. During invincibility, any enemy the player touches is instantly defeated (same as stomping) without the player taking damage. The player does not bounce — they run through enemies.
4. Show "+STAR" floating text at pickup location.
5. Add 300 points for pickup, plus 100 for each enemy defeated while invincible.

Track the invincibility on the player with a starTimer property. While starTimer > 0, the player cannot take damage and defeats enemies on contact. When starTimer reaches 0, return to normal vulnerability and normal colors.

The rainbow flash should override the normal damage-invincibility flash (which is just blinking).
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- Collecting a star power-up makes the player invincible for 8 seconds
- STAR_DURATION = 8 seconds is defined in constants.ts
- Player sprite cycles through rainbow colors rapidly during star power
- Touching enemies while invincible defeats them instantly
- Player does not take damage from enemies during star power
- Player does not bounce when defeating enemies via star (runs through)
- "+STAR" floating text appears at pickup location
- 300 points for star pickup, 100 per enemy defeated while active
- Star effect wears off after the timer expires
- Rainbow flash is visually distinct from damage invincibility flash

features.title: Size-up power-up makes player grow larger and extra life adds one life
features.description:
Implement two simpler power-up effects:

**Size-Up (red mushroom):**
When collected, the player grows from 16x16 to 16x32 (double height). This is tracked with a player.isBig boolean. When big:
- The player sprite changes to a taller version (define a 16x32 big player sprite in sprites.ts with the same color scheme but taller proportions)
- The player's collision box height doubles to 32
- The player's y position adjusts so they stand at the same ground level (subtract 16 from y)
- When the big player takes damage, instead of losing a life, they shrink back to small size and get the normal invincibility frames. This acts as a free hit.
- Show "+BIG" floating text at pickup. Add 200 points.

**Extra Life (green mushroom):**
When collected:
- Increment player lives by 1
- Show "+1UP" floating text at pickup in green
- Play a distinct visual flash (the entire screen briefly flashes white for 0.05 seconds)
- Add 0 points (the life itself is the reward)
- Lives can go above 3 (no cap)

Both power-ups are collected via the same AABB overlap mechanism as the speed boost.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- Collecting size-up mushroom makes the player double height (16x32)
- A taller big-player sprite is defined in sprites.ts
- Big player collision box is 16x32
- Big player stands at the same ground level after growing
- Big player shrinks instead of losing a life when damaged
- Shrinking grants invincibility frames
- "+BIG" floating text appears on size-up collection
- Collecting extra life mushroom adds 1 to lives
- Lives can exceed 3 (no maximum cap)
- "+1UP" floating text appears in green on life collection
- Screen briefly flashes on extra life pickup

features.title: Web Audio API chiptune background music loop plays during gameplay
features.description:
Create an audio system at lib/game/audio.ts using the Web Audio API. This module generates authentic 8-bit chiptune sounds using oscillator nodes — no audio files needed.

Create an AudioManager class with an AudioContext. CRITICAL: do NOT create the AudioContext on page load. Create it lazily on the first user interaction (when they press a key to insert a coin), and call audioCtx.resume() to comply with browser autoplay policies.

Implement a background music system:
1. Define a simple melody as an array of note objects: [{freq: number, duration: number}]. Use a catchy 8-16 note loop in a major key. Example notes: C4=262, D4=294, E4=330, F4=349, G4=392, A4=440, B4=494, C5=523.
2. Use a square wave oscillator for the melody (authentic NES sound).
3. Add a simple bass line on a second oscillator using a triangle wave at lower octave.
4. Loop the melody continuously during the PLAYING state.
5. Stop the music when leaving PLAYING state (pause, game over, etc.).

Connect oscillators through a GainNode to control volume. Set master volume to 0.15 so it is not overbearing. Add a playMusic() and stopMusic() method to AudioManager.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- lib/game/audio.ts exports an AudioManager class
- AudioContext is created lazily on first user interaction
- audioCtx.resume() is called on user interaction
- Background music plays during the PLAYING state
- Music uses square wave oscillator for melody (authentic 8-bit)
- Music uses triangle wave for bass line
- Melody loops continuously
- Music stops when leaving PLAYING state
- Master volume is set low (0.15) via GainNode
- playMusic() and stopMusic() methods control music playback
- No audio errors in the console

features.title: Player sound effects for jump, land, damage, and power-up pickup
features.description:
Add player-related sound effects to the AudioManager in lib/game/audio.ts. All sounds are generated programmatically using Web Audio API oscillators — no audio files.

Implement these sound effect methods:

1. playJump(): A quick ascending frequency sweep. Square wave, frequency ramps from 200Hz to 600Hz over 0.15 seconds, volume fades out. This creates the classic platformer jump chirp.

2. playLand(): A very short low thud. Triangle wave at 80Hz for 0.05 seconds. Subtle but provides feedback.

3. playDamage(): A descending buzz. Square wave from 400Hz down to 100Hz over 0.3 seconds, slightly louder than other effects. Sounds alarming.

4. playPowerUp(): An ascending arpeggio. Play 4 quick notes in sequence (C5, E5, G5, C6 = 523, 659, 784, 1047 Hz), each 0.08 seconds long with a square wave. Creates a triumphant pickup jingle.

Call these methods from the appropriate gameplay code: playJump() when the player jumps, playLand() when onGround transitions from false to true, playDamage() when the player takes damage, playPowerUp() when collecting any power-up.

All sound effects should respect the master volume. Each effect creates a new oscillator that auto-disconnects after playing.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- AudioManager has playJump(), playLand(), playDamage(), playPowerUp() methods
- Jump sound is an ascending frequency sweep (200Hz to 600Hz)
- Land sound is a short low thud
- Damage sound is a descending alarming buzz
- Power-up sound is an ascending arpeggio of 4 notes
- All sounds use Web Audio API oscillators (no audio files)
- Jump sound plays when player jumps
- Land sound plays when player lands on ground
- Damage sound plays when player takes damage
- Power-up sound plays when collecting any power-up
- Sounds respect the master volume level

features.title: World sound effects for coin collect, brick break, enemy stomp, and game over
features.description:
Add world/game-event sound effects to the AudioManager. All generated with Web Audio API oscillators.

Implement these methods:

1. playCoinCollect(): A quick two-note chime. Square wave plays B5 (988Hz) for 0.06s, then E6 (1319Hz) for 0.1s. Classic coin ding.

2. playBrickBreak(): A crunchy noise burst. Use an oscillator at 150Hz with high gain for 0.08 seconds, combined with a noise-like effect (rapidly alternating frequencies). Sounds like something shattering.

3. playEnemyStomp(): A satisfying bop. Square wave at 300Hz for 0.05s then 150Hz for 0.05s. A quick downward two-tone pop.

4. playGameOver(): A sad descending melody. Play a sequence of descending notes: G4(392), E4(330), C4(262), A3(220), each 0.25 seconds, square wave. A slow, dramatic descent.

5. playInsertCoin(): A metallic coin-drop sound. Triangle wave at 1200Hz for 0.03s, then 800Hz for 0.05s, then 600Hz for 0.08s. Three quick descending tones simulating a coin sliding into a slot.

Call these from the appropriate code: playCoinCollect() on coin pickup, playBrickBreak() on brick destruction, playEnemyStomp() on enemy stomp, playGameOver() on GAME_OVER state entry, playInsertCoin() on coin insertion.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- AudioManager has playCoinCollect, playBrickBreak, playEnemyStomp, playGameOver, playInsertCoin methods
- Coin collect is a quick two-note chime
- Brick break sounds crunchy or percussive
- Enemy stomp is a satisfying downward pop
- Game over is a slow descending sad melody
- Insert coin sounds metallic with descending tones
- All sounds use Web Audio API oscillators
- Each sound plays at the correct gameplay moment
- Sounds are distinct from each other and recognizable
- All sounds respect master volume

features.title: Title screen with game logo and flashing INSERT COIN text
features.description:
Implement the ATTRACT state title screen rendering. This is the first thing the player sees when loading the game.

The title screen should render:
1. The game title "RETRO ARCADE" in large pixel-art style text near the top of the screen. Render each letter as a block of filled rectangles to create a chunky pixel font effect, or use ctx.fillText with a large font size (at least 20px). Use a bright color like white or yellow (#FFD700).

2. Below the title, render a smaller subtitle or tagline like "A CLASSIC ADVENTURE" in a dimmer color.

3. In the lower third of the screen, render "INSERT COIN" text that flashes on and off. Toggle visibility every 0.5 seconds using a timer. When visible, render in bright white. This text should be prominently visible.

4. At the very bottom, small text: "PRESS ANY KEY" in gray.

5. Background: use the dark blue background (#0a0a2e) with the star field from the parallax background if available, or just a solid dark color.

The ATTRACT state should not accept gameplay input — only the coin insertion key (handled in the next feature). No enemies, player, or level should be visible on this screen.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- ATTRACT state renders a complete title screen
- "RETRO ARCADE" title is prominently displayed in large text
- Title text is bright and uses pixel-art styling or large font
- A subtitle appears below the main title
- "INSERT COIN" text flashes on/off every 0.5 seconds
- "PRESS ANY KEY" small text appears at the bottom
- Background is dark with optional star field
- No gameplay elements (player, enemies, level) are visible
- The title screen looks like a real arcade attract screen
- Timer-based flashing is smooth and consistent

features.title: Press key to insert coin with coin sound and transition to gameplay
features.description:
Implement the coin insertion mechanic that transitions from the ATTRACT state to the PLAYING state.

In the ATTRACT state update, listen for any key press using the InputManager's wasKeyPressed. When any key is pressed:

1. Play the insertCoin sound effect (the metallic coin-drop sound from the audio system)
2. Initialize the AudioContext if it has not been created yet (this is the first user interaction, required for Web Audio API)
3. Display a brief "CREDIT: 1" text on screen for 0.5 seconds
4. After the brief credit display, transition to PLAYING state

When transitioning to PLAYING:
1. Reset the game state: score = 0, coins = 0, lives = STARTING_LIVES
2. Reset the player position to the level startPosition
3. Reset player state: vx = 0, vy = 0, isBig = false, all power-up timers = 0
4. Reset all enemies to their initial positions and set active = true
5. Restore any broken bricks and used question blocks in the tile map (reload level data)
6. Reset all coins to their initial positions
7. Start the background music

This full reset ensures each new game starts fresh. The coin insertion should feel like a real arcade — satisfying click/coin sound, brief credit display, then game begins.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- Pressing any key during ATTRACT state triggers coin insertion
- Insert coin sound effect plays on key press
- AudioContext is initialized on this first interaction
- "CREDIT: 1" text briefly displays on screen
- Game transitions from ATTRACT to PLAYING state
- Score, coins, and lives are reset to starting values
- Player position and state are fully reset
- All enemies respawn at initial positions
- Level tiles are restored (broken bricks, used question blocks)
- Background music starts when gameplay begins
- Coins are reset to initial positions

features.title: Demo mode plays the game automatically via scripted input replay
features.description:
Implement a demo mode that plays on the title screen, making the game look alive while waiting for a player to insert a coin. This is the classic arcade attract mode where the game plays itself.

Create a demo system at lib/game/demo.ts. Define a scripted input sequence as an array of timed input commands: [{frame: number, keys: string[]}]. For example: [{frame: 0, keys: ['ArrowRight']}, {frame: 60, keys: ['ArrowRight', ' ']}, {frame: 90, keys: ['ArrowRight']}, ...]

The demo input sequence should:
1. Move the player right through the first portion of the level
2. Jump over a gap
3. Jump to hit a question block
4. Stomp an enemy
5. Collect some coins
6. Run for about 15-20 seconds total before looping

In the ATTRACT state, after the title screen has been displayed for 5 seconds with no input, start the demo mode. Switch the render to show the actual gameplay (level, player, enemies) but with input driven by the scripted sequence instead of the keyboard. Overlay "INSERT COIN" flashing text on top of the demo gameplay.

If the player presses a key during the demo, immediately stop the demo and proceed with coin insertion. The demo should loop back to the title screen after completing its sequence, creating a cycle: Title -> Demo -> Title -> Demo ...

Use a stripped-down game state for the demo — no HUD needed, no lives/score tracking.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- Demo mode starts automatically after 5 seconds of idle on title screen
- Demo shows actual gameplay with level, player, and enemies
- Player movement is driven by a scripted input sequence, not keyboard
- Demo player moves right, jumps, stomps enemies, and collects items
- "INSERT COIN" text flashes on top of the demo gameplay
- Pressing any key during demo stops it and triggers coin insertion
- Demo loops back to title screen after completing its sequence
- Attract mode cycles: Title -> Demo -> Title -> Demo
- Demo runs for approximately 15-20 seconds per loop
- Demo does not affect the actual game state (score, lives, etc.)

features.title: High score table displayed as part of attract mode cycle
features.description:
Extend the attract mode cycle to include a high score table display. The attract mode should now cycle through three screens:

1. Title screen (5 seconds) — the existing "RETRO ARCADE" / "INSERT COIN" screen
2. High score table (5 seconds) — shows the top 10 scores
3. Demo mode (15-20 seconds) — the scripted gameplay demo
4. Back to title screen, repeat

The high score table screen should render:
- "HIGH SCORES" header in large yellow text at the top
- A numbered list of the top 10 entries, each showing: rank (1-10), 3-letter initials, and zero-padded score
- Use a monospace font for alignment. Each row is evenly spaced vertically.
- If fewer than 10 entries exist, fill remaining rows with "---" and "000000"
- "INSERT COIN" flashing text at the bottom
- The same dark background as the title screen

Example layout:
```
     HIGH SCORES
  1. AAA  012500
  2. BOB  008200
  3. CAT  005100
  ...  
 10. ---  000000

   INSERT COIN
```

Pressing any key on any attract screen triggers coin insertion.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- Attract mode cycles through three screens: title, high scores, demo
- Each screen displays for its designated duration before advancing
- High score table shows rank, initials, and score for top 10 entries
- Scores are zero-padded to 6 digits
- Missing entries show dashes and zeros
- Table uses monospace alignment for clean columns
- "HIGH SCORES" header is displayed in yellow
- "INSERT COIN" flashes at the bottom of the high score screen
- Pressing any key during high score display triggers coin insertion
- High scores are loaded from localStorage via the highscores module

features.title: CRT screen curvature and bulge effect via CSS
features.description:
Add a CRT screen curvature effect that makes the game canvas look like an old curved CRT monitor. This is a purely visual CSS effect applied to the canvas container — do NOT modify the canvas rendering or use getImageData/putImageData.

In the ArcadeCabinet component (or a new CRTOverlay component), wrap the canvas in a container div with the class 'crt-screen'. Apply CSS to create the curvature:

1. Use border-radius with different horizontal and vertical values to create a barrel distortion look: border-radius: 20px / 30px (more curve on the vertical axis)
2. Apply overflow: hidden on the container so the canvas corners are clipped by the rounded edges
3. Add a subtle CSS perspective transform to enhance the 3D bulge: transform: perspective(800px) rotateX(1deg) — very slight, just enough to suggest depth
4. Add a subtle box-shadow inward: box-shadow: inset 0 0 30px rgba(0,0,0,0.5) to darken the edges and enhance the curved look

The effect should be subtle enough to not distort gameplay but visible enough to evoke a CRT screen. The canvas content itself must not be modified — all effects are CSS-only on the wrapper element.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- Canvas is wrapped in a container with CRT curvature styling
- Border-radius creates a barrel distortion effect on the screen edges
- Overflow hidden clips canvas corners to the curved shape
- A subtle perspective transform adds 3D depth
- Inward box-shadow darkens the edges
- The curvature is visible but does not significantly distort gameplay
- All effects are CSS-only, no canvas pixel manipulation
- The effect looks like a curved CRT monitor
- No performance impact from the CSS effects

features.title: Scanline overlay using CSS repeating-linear-gradient
features.description:
Add horizontal scanlines over the game canvas to simulate a CRT display. This is a CSS-only effect using a pseudo-element or an overlay div — do NOT modify canvas rendering.

Create a scanline overlay using one of these approaches:
1. A ::after pseudo-element on the CRT screen container
2. Or a separate div positioned absolutely over the canvas

The overlay should:
- Cover the entire canvas area
- Use pointer-events: none so it does not intercept mouse/touch events
- Have a repeating-linear-gradient background that creates horizontal lines: background: repeating-linear-gradient(0deg, transparent, transparent 1px, rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.15) 1px, rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.15) 2px). This creates alternating transparent and semi-transparent dark lines at 2px intervals.
- Be positioned with the same border-radius as the CRT screen container so the scanlines follow the curved shape
- Have a z-index higher than the canvas but lower than any UI elements

The scanlines should be subtle — they should darken the image slightly to give a CRT feel without making the game hard to see. The opacity value (0.15) can be adjusted for the right balance.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- Horizontal scanlines are visible over the game canvas
- Scanlines use CSS repeating-linear-gradient (no canvas manipulation)
- Lines alternate between transparent and semi-dark at 2px intervals
- Scanline overlay does not intercept pointer events (pointer-events: none)
- Scanlines follow the curved shape of the CRT screen
- Scanlines are subtle and do not obscure gameplay
- The overlay z-index is above the canvas
- Scanlines cover the entire canvas area
- No performance impact from the CSS overlay

features.title: Color bleed, vignette darkening, and subtle flicker via CSS
features.description:
Add three additional CRT visual effects using CSS only — no canvas pixel manipulation.

**1. Color Bleed / Chromatic Aberration:**
Apply a very subtle color fringing effect. On the CRT screen container, add a faint text-shadow or box-shadow with slight red and blue offsets: box-shadow: inset 2px 0 4px rgba(255,0,0,0.05), inset -2px 0 4px rgba(0,0,255,0.05). This creates a barely-visible red/blue fringe at the edges, simulating phosphor misalignment.

**2. Vignette (edge darkening):**
Add a radial-gradient overlay that darkens the corners and edges of the screen. Create a ::before pseudo-element (or additional overlay div) with: background: radial-gradient(ellipse at center, transparent 50%, rgba(0,0,0,0.4) 100%). This creates a natural darkening toward the edges, like a real CRT.

**3. Subtle Screen Flicker:**
Add a CSS @keyframes animation on the CRT screen container that subtly varies opacity between 0.97 and 1.0, cycling every 0.1 seconds. This creates the barely-perceptible flicker of a CRT refresh. The animation: crt-flicker 0.1s infinite. Keep it very subtle so it does not cause discomfort.

All three effects combined with the existing curvature and scanlines should create a convincing CRT look.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- Subtle red/blue color fringe is visible at screen edges
- Vignette darkens the corners and edges of the screen
- Vignette uses radial-gradient from transparent center to dark edges
- Screen has a very subtle flicker animation
- Flicker cycles between opacity 0.97 and 1.0
- Flicker is subtle enough to not cause discomfort
- All effects are CSS-only (no canvas getImageData)
- Effects combine well with existing curvature and scanlines
- No significant performance impact
- The combined CRT effect is convincing but not overwhelming

features.title: Cabinet marquee with game title and decorative bezel frame around screen
features.description:
Add arcade cabinet framing elements around the CRT screen. The game currently shows just the CRT screen — add the surrounding cabinet elements that give it the look of a real arcade machine.

**Marquee (above the screen):**
Add a div above the CRT screen container that acts as the arcade marquee. It should:
- Have a gradient background (dark red to brighter red, or use a starfield pattern)
- Display "RETRO ARCADE" in bold, stylized text (large, maybe with a text-shadow glow effect)
- Have a slight convex shape (border-radius on the bottom edges)
- Be about 60-80px tall
- Have a subtle CSS glow/backlight effect: box-shadow: 0 0 20px rgba(255,50,50,0.3)

**Bezel (around the screen):**
Wrap the CRT screen in a bezel div that provides:
- A dark gray (#1a1a1a) to black gradient background
- Padding around the screen (10-15px on each side)
- Rounded corners (border-radius: 8px)
- A slight inset border or groove effect to simulate the beveled edge of a real cabinet
- Subtle side art hints: thin colored stripes or geometric patterns on the left and right edges of the bezel

The overall layout should be a vertical stack: marquee on top, bezel with screen in the middle. Center the entire cabinet on the page against the black background. The cabinet should not be wider than about 500-600px total.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- A marquee div displays above the CRT screen
- Marquee shows "RETRO ARCADE" in bold stylized text
- Marquee has a colored gradient background
- Marquee has a subtle glow/backlight effect
- A bezel div wraps the CRT screen with padding
- Bezel has a dark gray/black gradient background
- Bezel has rounded corners and an inset border effect
- Side art or decorative elements appear on the bezel
- The full cabinet is centered on the page
- Cabinet width is constrained to 500-600px maximum
- The overall look evokes a real arcade machine

features.title: Pause functionality with P or Escape key and pause overlay
features.description:
Implement pause/unpause functionality during gameplay. When the player presses P or Escape during the PLAYING state:

1. Transition to the PAUSED state
2. Stop the background music
3. Stop all game logic updates (enemies stop moving, player stops, physics pauses)
4. Render a pause overlay on top of the frozen game frame:
   - Semi-transparent dark overlay (rgba(0,0,0,0.6)) covering the canvas
   - Large white text "PAUSED" centered on screen
   - Smaller text below: "PRESS P TO RESUME" or "PRESS ESC TO RESUME"

When P or Escape is pressed again during the PAUSED state:
1. Transition back to PLAYING state
2. Resume background music
3. Resume all game logic

IMPORTANT: The deltaTime on the first frame after unpausing should NOT include the time spent paused. The game loop already caps deltaTime at 1/30, but verify this works correctly after a long pause.

Pause should be responsive — pressing P should instantly freeze the game with no delay.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- Pressing P during PLAYING state pauses the game
- Pressing Escape during PLAYING state pauses the game
- PAUSED state renders a dark semi-transparent overlay
- "PAUSED" text is prominently displayed and centered
- "PRESS P TO RESUME" instruction text is shown
- Background music stops when paused
- All game logic stops updating when paused
- Pressing P or Escape during PAUSED state resumes gameplay
- Background music resumes on unpause
- No physics jump or explosion on resume (deltaTime is handled)
- Pause is instantaneous with no delay

features.title: Game complete screen when player reaches level end goal with score summary
features.description:
Implement a level completion screen that displays when the player reaches the flagpole/goal at the end of the level. This replaces the temporary GAME_OVER transition that was used as a placeholder.

When the player reaches the goal:
1. Freeze player input — the player can no longer move
2. Play a brief victory animation: the player slides down the flagpole (if using a flagpole) or walks through the door, taking about 1.5 seconds
3. Stop the background music and play a short victory jingle (ascending notes: C5, E5, G5, C6, played over 1 second)
4. Transition to a completion screen that shows:
   - "LEVEL COMPLETE!" in large yellow text
   - Final score
   - Coins collected (e.g., "15/20 COINS")
   - Time bonus (optional: award extra points based on how quickly the level was completed)
   - Enemies defeated count
   - "PRESS ENTER TO CONTINUE" flashing text
5. When Enter is pressed, transition to HIGH_SCORE_ENTRY if the score qualifies, or ATTRACT if it does not

This is distinct from the game over screen — this is a positive, celebratory ending. Use brighter colors (gold, yellow) and a triumphant tone.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- Reaching the level goal triggers a completion sequence
- Player input is frozen during the completion
- A brief victory animation plays at the goal
- Background music stops and a victory jingle plays
- Completion screen shows "LEVEL COMPLETE!" in large yellow text
- Final score is displayed
- Coins collected count is shown
- Enemies defeated count is shown
- "PRESS ENTER TO CONTINUE" flashes at the bottom
- Enter transitions to HIGH_SCORE_ENTRY or ATTRACT based on score
- The screen feels celebratory with bright colors and positive tone

features.title: Project builds successfully with npm run build
features.description: Ensure the Next.js project compiles and builds without errors when running npm run build. The build output should complete with exit code 0. All TypeScript files should compile without type errors. The build should produce a .next directory with the compiled output. The package.json should have a "build" script configured. This verifies that the project structure, imports, and TypeScript configuration are all correct and production-ready.
features.acceptanceCriteria:
- Running npm run build completes with exit code 0
- No TypeScript compilation errors
- No missing module errors
- .next directory is created with build output
- package.json contains a "build" script
- All imports resolve correctly
